title: Anionic/Controlled Radical Polymerization
abstract: Several polymer architectures were synthesized using anionic and controlled radical polymerization. PVA-g-PEO bottle brush polymers were characterized by TEM. ATRP "grafting from" brushes showed bimodal molecular weight distributions at longer reaction times. α-methylstyrene was proposed as a terminator to narrow dispersity. Hydrogels were prepared via azeotropic initiation of ethylene oxide, and a passing-through polymerization method produced denser brushes than conventional grafting-from approaches.

title: Metal-Free, Self-Supporting Graphene Membrane Fabricated by Solvent Interface Trapping for Electrochemical CO₂ Reduction
blurb: We trap graphene inside a water purification membrane in a single step and discovered it moonlights as a CO₂-reducing catalyst, no precious metals required. Apply a small voltage, feed it carbon dioxide, and it converts greenhouse gas into formate and acetate—staying stable for over 16 hours, outperforming gold nanoparticles we deliberately added.
abstract: Electrochemical CO₂ reduction typically requires noble metal catalysts or heteroatom-doped carbon materials to achieve satisfactory activity. We report the electrochemical reduction of CO₂ using a self-supporting pristine graphene membrane fabricated by solvent interface trapping (SITM) without metal or dopant additives. The macroscopic membrane (10.7 cm²) demonstrated stable CO₂ reduction over 60,000 s at potentials ranging from 0.8 to 5.0 V, without performance degradation. Product analysis by ¹H NMR confirmed formate production across the examined potential range. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy revealed distributed RC network behavior, suggesting that nanometer-scale gaps between overlapping graphene sheets may concentrate the electric field, thereby facilitating CO₂ activation. This work establishes the reduction activity of pristine graphene when appropriately structured and provides a metal-free platform for CO₂ reduction applications.

title: Super-Strong, Tough yet Reversible Hydrogels Engineered Through Cationic Network Systems
abstract: Achieving a combination of high strength, toughness, reversibility, and fatigue resistance in hydrogels remains a challenge, as most current designs rely on irreversible or slowly recovering energy-dissipation mechanisms. Here, we report a versatile freeze–thawing, densification, and crosslinking (FDC) strategy that enables simultaneous enhancement of these mechanical properties by modulating polymer crystallinity and dynamic metal–oxygen coordination. Using polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) as a model system, we demonstrate that diverse metal cations (Li+, Zn2+, Fe3+) markedly increase crystalline domain formation and crosslinking density, yielding hydrogels with exceptional tensile strength (up to 64.7 MPa), toughness (up to 109.7 MJ m⁻³), and fracture energy (up to 44.2 kJ m⁻²). Remarkably, these hydrogels exhibit rapid and high reversibility (>95%) under cyclic loading with minimal hysteresis, and achieve outstanding fatigue thresholds up to 9.3 kJ m⁻². Molecular dynamics simulations reveal that the mechanical reinforcement arises from synergistic contributions of crystallization, metal–oxygen interactions, and densified chain packing. The FDC strategy is generalizable across multiple polymer systems and cation species, enabling broad tunability of mechanical properties and extending this platform to gelatin-, alginate-, and polyacrylamide-based hydrogels. This work establishes a general and programmable design framework for creating hydrogels that combine high mechanical robustness with dynamic reversibility, offering strong potential for applications in soft robotics, biomedical engineering, and wearable or implantable devices.

title: Small Angle X-ray Scattering (SAXS) Analysis of Modified PNA for Antisense
abstract: Peptide nucleic acid (PNA) is applicable as antisense for targeting complementary RNA or DNA. It has the advantages of high stability and efficacy yet suffers from low solubility. Here, we report a small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) analysis on the aggregation behavior and conformation of a series of PNA modified systems, which can target specific RNA. The morphology and antisense efficiency of modified PNAs are drastically different and seemingly strongly correlated. We will demonstrate SAXS can be a powerful tool to identify nanoscale morphology and determine the aggregation behavior.

title: Liquid-Based Encapsulation for Implantable Bioelectronics Across Broad pH Environment
blurb: What if implantable electronics could survive the body's harshest environments? Our liquid-based encapsulation technology protects flexible bioelectronics from water, ions, and extreme pH while maintaining mechanical compliance with tissues. This enables stable wireless device operation for long-term inside living systems.
abstract: Wearable and implantable bioelectronics that can interface for extended periods with highly mobile organs and tissues across a broad pH range would be useful for various applications in basic biomedical research and clinical medicine. The encapsulation of these systems, however, presents a major challenge, as such devices require superior barrier performance against water and ion penetration in challenging pH environments while also maintaining flexibility and stretchability to match the physical properties of the surrounding tissue. Current encapsulation materials are often limited to near neutral pH conditions, restricting their application range. In this work, we report a liquid-based encapsulation approach for bioelectronics under extreme pH environments. This approach achieves high optical transparency, stretchability, and mechanical durability. When applied to implantable wireless optoelectronic devices, our encapsulation method demonstrates outstanding water resistance in vitro, ranging from extremely acidic environments (pH = 1.5 and 4.5) to alkaline conditions (pH = 9). We also demonstrate the in vivo biocompatibility of our encapsulation approach and show that encapsulated wireless optoelectronics maintain robust operation throughout 3 months of implantation in freely moving mice. These results indicate that our encapsulation strategy has the potential to protect implantable bioelectronic devices in a wide range of research and clinical applications.

title: Polyamide Thin-Film Composite Membranes for Gas Separations
blurb: We spray polyamide into ultrathin membranes for gas separation, but at that thickness, tiny defects let everything through. Adding a thin silicone seal plugs those defects and recovers real selectivity, pushing H₂/CO₂ separation from near-zero to 5–20. A simple two-step fix for a problem that has limited ultrathin membranes for years.
abstract: The urgent need to mitigate greenhouse gas emissions and enable sustainable energy systems has intensified interest in membrane technologies for gas separation, which offer advantages such as lower energy consumption, high selectivity, scalability, and ease of integration compared to conventional methods. Applications include CO₂ capture, hydrogen purification, and natural gas upgrading, and a variety of materials—including carbon molecular sieves, zeolites, polyimides, polymers of intrinsic microporosity (PIMs), and block copolymers—have been explored. Despite their widespread use in reverse osmosis, polyamides remain relatively underexplored for gas separation, although tightened PA structures with reduced free volume and higher crosslink density can enhance intrinsic selectivity by restricting transport of larger gas molecules. However, conventional interfacial polymerization often produces relatively thick, rough films with limited control over microstructure, where defects can dominate gas transport. In this work, we investigate electrosprayed ultrathin polyamide membranes for gas separation. By tuning deposition cycles and monomer concentrations, selective-layer thickness and PA network structure were controlled. Pure-gas permeation results showed that electrosprayed PA membranes are highly sensitive to nanoscale defects, leading to Knudsen-type transport with high permeance (~10–100 GPU) but minimal selectivity. Applying a thin PDMS sealing layer suppressed nonselective pathways and revealed the intrinsic selectivity of the PA layer, decreasing hydrogen permeance to ~1–10 GPU and increasing H₂/CO₂ selectivity to ~5–20 depending on PA thickness and chemistry. These results highlight electrosprayed PA membranes as a promising platform for highly selective gas separations such as CO₂ capture and hydrogen purification.

title: Structure–Activity Relationships of Functionalized Cannabinoids for Antimicrobial Activity and Biofilm Inhibition
blurb: Medical devices like catheters frequently cause infections when bacteria form protective biofilms on their surfaces. Our work shows that certain modified cannabinoid molecules can retain antimicrobial activity, opening a path to making medical devices with built-in antimicrobial properties that fight infections.
abstract: Antimicrobial resistance and biofilm-associated infections caused by methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) demand new strategies that go beyond conventional antibiotics. Natural cannabinoids such as cannabidiol (CBD) and cannabinol (CBN) have emerged as promising anti-MRSA leads, but their development as functional biomaterials is limited by suboptimal physicochemical properties and incomplete structure–activity understanding. In this work, we synthesized a series of polymerizable and non-polymerizable CBD and CBN derivatives and systematically evaluated their antibacterial and antibiofilm effects against MRSA using broth microdilution, crystal violet biofilm staining, resazurin metabolic assays, and membrane integrity measurements. Select methacrylate derivatives retained or improved intrinsic activity, whereas other substitution patterns led to a marked loss of function. These findings reveal clear structure–activity trends linking cannabinoid scaffold modification to MRSA biofilm disruption and metabolic inhibition, and identify a small subset of polymerizable derivatives as particularly promising candidates for incorporation into antibiofilm materials.

title: Electrosprayed Membranes for Electrochemical Applications
blurb: Hidden inside the technologies we rely on for clean water and clean energy is something almost invisible, a tiny layer powerful enough to shape how efficiently the whole system works. It's a microscopic gatekeeper, quietly deciding what gets through and what stays behind. But in most devices today, this layer is far thicker than it should be, wasting both energy and money. My work reinvents this invisible piece, making it ultrathin so we can build cleaner, cheaper, and more efficient technologies for everyone.
abstract: Producing thin film composite polymeric membranes via electrospray has been demonstrated to be a promising approach to overcome several drawbacks present in traditional solution and extrusion casting processes. The drawbacks include the large consumption of expensive selective materials and/or solvents, as well as the production of thick films. Thick films result in significant electrical resistance and, consequently, high energy consumption in electrochemical systems, directly impacting the operating costs of such systems and limiting the application space. Employing electrospray as a membrane manufacturing technique allows the formation of thin film composite ion-exchange membranes with tailorable properties and composition, without requiring excessive use of materials. Moreover, this approach has the potential to be extended to commercial materials such as Nafion or nonfluorinated alternatives that may emerge as more sustainable solutions. This research focuses on the fabrication and characterization of printed thin-film composite Nafion membranes using electrospray, in which a commercial ionomer dispersion is electrosprayed to produce thin, low-resistance, and selective cation-exchange membranes. Here, we investigate the ability of electrospray-printed Nafion ionomer to form ultrathin film composite membranes for diverse applications, integrating its unique properties and the electrospray printing approach. This technique enables precise tuning of membrane properties by adjusting the thickness of the selective layer, and/or by changing the substrate, which significantly influences membrane performance. For example, a 5 µm-thick selective Nafion layer was formed on the surface of a porous substrate and thermally annealed, resulting in membranes with a permselectivity of 0.89 compared to N115 (127 µm, permselectivity: 0.94), a significant performance achievement given the membrane's reduced thickness.

title: Glass Transition Temperature for Entangled and Cross-Linked Polymer Melts in Molecular Dynamics Simulations
abstract: We study the effects of cross-linking and entanglements on the glass transition temperature of polymer melts using molecular dynamics (MD) simulations of Kremer-Grest polymers. Although the increase of the glass transition temperature from cross-linking is well documented in both experiment and simulation, entanglements have not shown the same increase in simulations. For this work, we create an entangled polymer melt in M-D by looping linear chains together for a total of 1 entanglement for every 2 chains. We also created a melt with 1 cross-link for every 2 chains as well as a control melt with no induced entanglements or cross-links. The result is a noticeable increase in Tg for the entangled and cross-linked case compared to the control, which aligns better with theoretical expectations of polymer melt glass transition.

title: Templating Mechanically Robust Polymer-Graphene High Internal Phase Emulsions
blurb: Squeezing an emulsion through a needle instead of shaking it by hand makes the bubbles uniform, and that uniformity nearly doubles the strength of our graphene-polystyrene foams. The best samples hit 8 MPa compressive strength and 160 MPa modulus, all from a porous material made with off-the-shelf graphite.
abstract: Lightweight structural materials with high mechanical performance require precise control over composite morphology. Here we present an emulsifying needle technique for fabricating polymer-graphene nanocomposites via the Solvent Interface Trapping Method (SITM), in which pristine graphite is exfoliated at an oil-water interface to stabilize water droplets in a hydrophobic monomer phase. Upon polymerization, these High Internal Phase Emulsions (HIPEs) yield porous polystyrene-graphene (PS-G) foams whose mechanical properties are strongly dictated by sphere size and uniformity. Compared to conventional hand-shaking, needle emulsification produces significantly more monodisperse droplet distributions (PDI reduced from ~0.35 to as low as 0.04), resulting in marked improvements in both compressive strength and Young's modulus across multiple formulations. These gains were further amplified by tuning the water-to-oil ratio, reducing graphite flake size from 10 um to 3-5 um, and increasing graphite loading to 4 w/w%. The highest compressive strengths observed exceeded 8 MPa with moduli approaching 160 MPa. These findings demonstrate that morphological control - rather than compositional change alone - is a powerful and scalable lever for engineering high-performance lightweight composites.

title: Designing Synthetic Block Copolypeptides for Solvent-Free Thermal Induction and Tunable β-Sheet Nanocrystals
abstract: Most synthetic polypeptide self-assembly and secondary-structure transitions rely on solvents, making them highly sensitive and difficult to process or scale. Here, we present a strategy for solvent-free α-helix to β-sheet transformations in block copolypeptides, achieving precise control over conformation while facilitating thermomechanical processing. Central to our design is the "sergeant–soldier" effect, wherein poly(γ-benzyl-L-glutamate) (PBLG) serves as a helicity-promoting scaffold (sergeant) that compels β-sheet–prone amino acid N-carboxyanhydrides (soldiers) to adopt stable helical conformations in the chain propagation, and maintain the structure through the synthesis and purification procedures. Under elevated temperatures, the sergeant block is plasticized in situ by a poly(ethylene glycol) domain, triggering a solvent-free helix-to-β-sheet transition. We further implement a two-stage compression molding protocol to induce liquid-crystal–like orientation, thereby increasing conversion efficiency. The resulting films exhibit enhanced mechanical extensibility through well-distributed β-sheet nanocrystals embedded in the helical matrix. Interestingly, incorporating a random copolypeptide block with amino acids of varying side-chain flexibility demonstrates how π-stacking and side-chain complementarity can optimize these solvent-free α-helix to β-sheet transitions. Overall, this work provides a robust framework for engineering thermally responsive block copolypeptides with well-defined β-sheet nanocrystals—expanding the possibilities for functional biomaterials and advanced polypeptide architectures.

title: Synthesis and Characterization of Sustainable Gels with Enhanced Mechanical Robustness
blurb: We conjugate liquid crystals onto cellulose nanocrystals, then crosslink them with a cattle protein - all dissolved in an ionic liquid. The result is a sustainable, mechanically robust gel that incorporates 15× more nanocrystals than previously thought compatible with protein matrices, opening the door to next-generation biomedical materials.
abstract: The development of mechanically robust, sustainable gels for biomedical applications remains a significant challenge, particularly in balancing flexibility, compatibility, and functionality. This work presents the synthesis and characterization of composite gels derived from bovine serum albumin (BSA) and cellulose nanocrystals conjugated with a thermotropic liquid crystalline moiety (CNC-COO-CB12). Rheological characterization via amplitude and frequency sweep analyses demonstrated viscoelastic gel behavior, while FTIR and DSC analyses confirmed successful crosslinking and thermal properties of the fabricated gels. Notably, a CNC-containing unit concentration of 7.5 wt% was achieved, representing a substantial improvement over the previously reported compatibility limit of 0.5 wt% CNCs in protein matrices. These findings demonstrate that exploiting synergistic interactions between CNC-COO-CB12 and BSA enables the fabrication of mechanically robust gels with promising potential for biomedical applications.

title: 3D Printed Self-Powered Piezoelectric Smart Scaffolds for Enhanced Bone Tissue Regeneration
blurb: This smart 3D-printed scaffold wakes up under ultrasound, producing electrical signals and releasing bone-building minerals that help the body regenerate bone.
abstract: Piezoelectricity in native bones has been recognized as a key factor in bone regeneration. Piezoelectric materials can mimic this effect by providing electrical stimulation that recruits host cells, enhances osteogenic differentiation, and promotes mineralization. To further strengthen their regenerative potential, calcium phosphate (CaP) is often incorporated to support osteoconductivity and stimulate the secretion of endogenous bone-growth factors, thereby creating a beneficial osteogenic microenvironment. Nevertheless, conventional fabrication methods face significant challenges in producing personalized piezoelectric scaffolds with both functional and structural precision. Extrusion based three-dimensional (3D) printing uses layer-by-layer deposition to customize scaffold geometry and induces molecular chain alignment through heating and stretching during extrusion, which promotes the formation of the beta-phase crystalline structure and enhances piezoelectricity. In this study, we developed 3D printed piezoelectric smart scaffolds composed of poly(L-lactic acid) (PLLA) blended with beta-tricalcium phosphate (beta-TCP) by extrusion-based 3D printing, with a uniform pore size of ~200 um with high interconnectivity. These 3D printed piezoelectric smart scaffolds can be remotely activated by ultrasound to promote bone regeneration through the synergistic effects of piezoelectric self-charging and the osteo-inductive ions (e.g., Ca2+ and PO43-) released during beta-TCP degradation.

title: Thermo-Responsive Conductive Double Network Hydrogel via Solvent Interfacial Trapping Method
blurb: A hydrogel that conducts electricity through graphene networks and changes its sensing ability with temperature. At body temperature it becomes 7x more sensitive to pressure, so a single patch could detect both a finger press and a fever.
abstract: We report a novel graphene-based double network (DN) hydrogel fabricated through the solvent interface trapping method. Graphene-coated poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) (g-PNIPAM) spheres are densely packed within a poly(N-(hydroxymethyl)acrylamide) (PHMA) matrix, forming a percolating conductive network via graphene-coated interfaces while retaining thermoresponsive properties. The DN architecture preserves PNIPAM's thermoresponsive behavior near its LCST (~37C) while providing dimensional stability through ~4-5x suppression of swelling. The material exhibits coupled thermo-mechanical-electrical response, with temperature-tunable piezoresistive sensitivity and gauge factors increasing from 1.2 at 25C to 8.5 at 45C, making it a promising dual-mode sensing platform for wearable and biomedical applications.

title: Polymer Walks on Weakly Adsorbing Spherical Surfaces
blurb: Micro-/Nanoemulsions behave in counter-intuitive ways and pose challenges in commercial applications such as petroleum refining and beyond. We are analyzing the mechanisms of nanodroplet/polymer interactions and nanodroplet aggregation for a deeper physical understanding.
abstract: We analyzed systems of polyethylene oxide (PEO) polymers adsorbed on nanodroplets of octane and octanoic acid. We find an agreement in behavior with results expected for colloid and nanoparticle analogs. Computer simulation was used to quantify the parameters of the system. Precise description of behavior is achieved through atomistic molecular dynamics. Hamiltonian Monte Carlo simulation then allows for comparison with an idealized limit of strong adhesion to spherical surfaces. We find that the size of droplets in relation to the radius of gyration of the polymer has a great impact on the degree of adhesion, with increases in droplet size, temperature, and presence of functional groups capable of hydrogen bonding in droplets increasing the strength of adhesion. For systems of multiple nanodroplets, the aggregation of droplets dependent on and/or aided by the presence of PEO was studied. Findings have implications for demulsification crucial in the petroleum industry, nanoemulsion processing, and beyond.

title: Triple-Layered Core-Shell Microneedle Patch for Sustained Analgesic Delivery and Chondroprotection in Osteoarthritis
blurb: Managing arthritis pain usually means pills every day or injections at the clinic. Our microneedle patch goes on once, plants invisible needles under the skin, and delivers two pain drugs for 10 days straight. No daily doses, no clinic visits, no bulky bandages.
abstract: Osteoarthritis (OA) is a prevalent chronic condition characterized by persistent pain, necessitating sustained therapeutic interventions to manage symptoms effectively. In this study, we introduce a novel core-shell microneedle system for sustained release, termed Microneedle-based Integrated Release with Advanced Composite Layered Efficacy (MIRACLE). The design is a triple-layered microneedle (MN) patch engineered for the prolonged management of OA-associated pain. The patch consists of a 15 x 15 array within a 1 cm2 area, featuring a specialized three-level architecture. One core-layer of the MIRACLE is designed to provide sustained controlled release of multiple pain drugs. One sub-shell layer is designed to prevent premature burst release (over-dosing) of the encapsulated payloads and an outermost layer is designed to ensure sufficient mechanical strength for skin penetration regardless of the core-layer drug formulation which is often tuned to obtain different sustained release profiles. Within an hour of application, an integrated effervescent layer (between the needle tips and backing layer) is rapidly dissolved and facilitates implantation of the MNs into the skin. Subsequently, the system provides a controlled, co-delivery of Bupivacaine (BUP) and Meloxicam (MEL), which act synergistically to provide sustained anesthetic and anti-inflammatory effects for up to 10 days (for comparison, the only commercial long-acting Zynrelef formulation for post-operational pain only has up to 3-day acting period) in OA rat models. Besides the analgesic efficacy, the MIRACLE system demonstrated a significant capacity to mitigate articular cartilage degradation. This multi-layered MNs platform could represent an excellent strategy for not only the treatment of chronic osteoarthritis pain but also the management of other types of neuromusculoskeletal pains, offering a significant and broad impact on modern medicine.

title: Polymer Ligand-Engineered Nanoparticle Interfaces for Selective Ion Transport to Enhance CO2 Electroreduction
blurb: We created a "smart coating" for tiny metal particles, like giving them a stylish jacket that boosts performance. The coating has charged "sticky heads" that pull helpful molecules (like bicarbonate) right to the surface where reactions happen, while the rest acts like a traffic controller for water and ions. The result? Smoother reactions, higher faradaic efficiency over 90% conversion of CO₂ into useful fuel materials.
abstract: We report a new class of hydrophobic polymer ligands with quaternary ammonium head groups for modifying noble metal nanoparticles. These ligands attach through electrostatic interactions, producing polymer-grafted nanoparticles with high colloidal and chemical stability. The charged head groups enrich bicarbonate ions near the catalyst surface, while the hydrophobic polymer backbone restructures the local hydrogen-bonding environment, improving ion and water transport. These interfacial effects enhance CO2 electroreduction, achieving CO Faradaic efficiencies above 90%.

title: Preparation of Thiol-Ene Functionalized Polymer-Grafted Silk Nanoparticles
blurb: Silkworm silk is processed into noncytotoxic nanoparticles. Polymethacrylates are grown directly from the surface and modified using versatile thiol-ene click chemistry. The graft and end group can be tailored to achieve desirable surface properties for use in cell targeting and drug delivery.
abstract: Silk is a biopolymer that is produced at commodity scale due to its utility for the textile industry. Regenerated silk fibroin is isolated and purified from silk to make biocompatible and biodegradable hydrogels, sponges, films, particles, and other materials for tissue engineering and regenerative medicine applications. Due to their small size, nanoparticle systems have the potential to leverage cellular uptake mechanisms for drug delivery, cell targeting, or imaging applications. Silk particles can be formulated to be stable in vivo yet ultimately degrade, which may offer an advantage over metallic particles that do not readily degrade and can accumulate in tissue over time. This work presents silk nanoparticles enhanced with surface-grafted methacrylate polymers which are then end-functionalized using click chemistry. Silk nanoparticles are formed by nanoprecipitation in acetone and characterized by multi-angle dynamic light scattering. A RAFT chain transfer agent is then attached to the hydroxyl-enhanced surface to enable polymerization of methacrylate monomers. Polymer grafting is confirmed by ATR-FTIR and Raman spectroscopy and graft length is defined by NMR and GPC. Grafts are then end-functionalized with various molecules using thiol-ene click chemistry. Nanoparticles are confirmed noncytotoxic by incubating with human fibroblasts in vitro and assaying using MTS. This system presents a platform for customizable nanoparticles with tunable polymer grafts and end group identity for enhanced cell targeting applications.

title: Development of Synthetic Polypeptide Mediated Lipid Nano-Discs
blurb: We synthesize amphiphilic copolypeptides to self-assemble stable lipid/polypeptide nanodiscs (20-50 nm) robustly over a wide temperature range, independent of lipid phases. The novel lipid/polypeptide nanodiscs have great potential for biomedical applications and biophysical science.
abstract: Lipid nanodiscs provide a controlled membrane geometry with relevance to nanomedicine, biophysics of membrane proteins, drug delivery, and biodiagnosis. Their high aspect ratio and nanoscale dimensions preserve a planar bilayer core and mimic the local structure of cell membranes. One type of lipid nanodisc, bicelles, have demonstrated a superior cellular uptake compared to liposomes of the same composition. Nevertheless, bicelles are thermodynamically fragile, readily destabilized upon dilution, variation of pH or temperature. Lipid nanodiscs can also be made by membrane scaffold proteins or synthetic copolymers, e.g., poly(styrene-co-maleic acid), while the former requires a complicated procedure to be purified and the latter is biologically irrelevant. We have synthesized a lipid nanodisc platform stabilized by synthetic amphiphilic copolypeptides with high biological relevance. Amphiphilicity arises from the statistical incorporation of hydrophobic and charged residues along the polypeptide backbone. In aqueous environments, these polymers wrap around the nanodisc rim and stabilize lipid bilayer with fragments 20-50 nm in diameter. The formation of nanodiscs is robust across variations in polymer molecular weight and composition. Significantly, unlike bicelles which only form at gel-phase (liquid ordered) lipids, we obtain the nanodiscs over a wide temperature range with both gel- and fluid- phase lipids. This platform maintains structural uniformity while preserving lipid and polymer composition as tunable parameters. As a result, the size, surface charge, and membrane rigidity of the novel lipid nanodiscs can be designed and engineered independently for different applications.

title: Chiral Monooxygenation in Microemulsions Using Cu-Albumin Artificial Enzyme
blurb: We build enzymes without enzymes. By anchoring a copper catalyst inside a readily available protein, we harness its natural chiral environment to drive selective monooxygenation—combining the precision of biology with the simplicity of synthetic chemistry.
abstract: Inspired by natural monooxygenases, we engineered a copper-bovine serum albumin (Cu-BSA) artificial enzyme that exhibits dual oxidase (chiral-selective) and peroxidase activities with significant electrochemical properties. The Cu2+/Cu+ redox cycle facilitates efficient O2 reduction to H2O2 (>85% Faradaic efficiency), which supports >99% enantioselective styrene epoxidation, facilitating cascade reaction in mild aqueous conditions. This artificial enzymatic system highlights the versatility of noncatalytic proteins in engineering artificial enzymes, showcasing significant potential for chiral-selective transformations of non-natural compounds.

title: Interfacial Self-Assembly of Graphene Networks at 1 vol% Enables Piezoresistive and Electrothermal Silicone Foams
blurb: We shake graphite, silicone, and water in a jar for one minute. The graphene coats the water droplets, silicone cures around them as the walls, and when the water dries out you're left with a conductive sponge. Under 2% filler, senses your heartbeat, heats to 143 C, and stays squishy because the silicone walls themselves are basically filler-free.
abstract: Segregated network composites achieve electrical percolation at minimal filler loadings by localizing conductivity to architectural boundaries. However, achieving this architecture typically requires tedious multi-step processing, often incompatible with scalable manufacturing. Here, we report the single-step synthesis of a silicone composite segregated network composed of self-assembled percolating graphene networks. These networks are templated by graphene-stabilized water-in-oil emulsions, created by spontaneous exfoliation of graphite at oil/water interfaces generated by agitation. After polymerization of the silicone and removal of the dispersed aqueous phase, the resulting foams achieve conductivities of 0.43 S/m at a 1.37 vol% loading of graphene. This is a 12-fold reduction compared to prior interfacial assembly methods, which require 16.5 vol% loading and thousands of hours of processing to achieve comparable electrical conductivity. Architectural segregation decouples mechanical and electrical properties, enabling application-specific modulus tuning. The foams exhibit reversible piezoresistance with a U-shaped strain response, are stable through 5000 cycles, and provide voltage-controlled heating to 143 C at milliamp current densities, three orders of magnitude lower than those of continuous CVD graphene networks. The low thermal mass of the air-filled foam structure provides rapid thermal response, achieving 28-second de-icing from -17 C.

title: Scale-Up of High-Temperature Polyolefins for Dielectrics, Film Processing, and Testing
abstract: Dielectric polymers used in electrical and electronic systems require withstanding high electric fields and elevated temperature. Modified o-polyoxachloronorbornene (o-POClNB) chosen for scaling up as they demonstrated excellent dielectric performance at high electric field and high temperature (between 150°C to 200°C) operating range. These polymers have also high enough molecular weight to be able to make entanglements in polymer chains. Polymer samples were used for mechanical testing and solution processing for film casting. The large-scale polymer films would undergo further extensive dielectric testing. The thermal, mechanical and dielectric properties of these polymers showed the possible melt processibility when synthesized at a larger scale. Scaling up these polymers would help in understanding cost, evaluation of morphology and structure property relationship with dielectric and thermal properties for high energy density capacitors and other energy storage devices. Replacement of biaxially oriented polypropylene (BOPP) for capacitor application is very important especially at operational temperature above 100°C with potential impacts over broad range of NAVY applications in pulse power and marine electronics.

title: TBA
